one_line_takeaway: A compact, LLM-inclusive taxonomy and evaluation-metric catalog for machine unlearning that is useful as an orientation map and a critique of weak unlearning evaluation, but offers no experiments or quantitative comparisons of its own.
key_finding: The survey's own analytical conclusion is that current unlearning evaluation is immature and risks 'arbitrary forgetting': every empirical metric it catalogs has a documented drawback (e.g., accuracy on D_u cannot by itself confirm sensitive data was removed; membership-inference-based checks damage model performance; the ZRF score is contested for LLM unlearning; the theory-based Anamnesis Index only works in the zero-shot setting). It stresses that no single metric suffices and comprehensive, standardized evaluation is needed. For LLM unlearning specifically it observes (citing the TOFU benchmark from [39]) that parameter-optimization results remain 'frustrating,' i.e., these methods still need improvement. It distinguishes exact unlearning (indistinguishable distribution vs. retrained model; strong privacy but hard/costly) from approximate unlearning (cheaper, generalizable, but cannot guarantee removal of sensitive parameters).
core_contribution: A single-author 'short survey' (Yi Xu, Renmin University of China) that proposes a unified taxonomy of machine unlearning spanning both traditional ML models and Large Language Models. Its self-claimed novelty over prior unlearning surveys (it explicitly positions itself against [62] Xu et al. 2023 'Machine Unlearning: A Survey' and [34] Li et al. 2024, whose notation it adopts) is extending the taxonomy to cover LLM-specific unlearning. The taxonomy has four branches: (1) data-driven MU (data influence/poisoning, data partition, data augmentation); (2) model-based MU (model shifting, model pruning, model replacement); (3) parameter-tuning LLM unlearning (parameter optimization, parameter merging); (4) parameter-agnostic LLM unlearning (in-context unlearning / ICuL). It also organizes evaluation criteria into empirical (time-, accuracy-, similarity-, attack-based) and theoretical (theory-based) families, and frames unlearning 'targets' as exact vs. approximate unlearning. The contribution is organizational/definitional, not experimental.
threat_model: The paper is genuinely a security/privacy paper (CCS concept: 'Security and privacy'). The motivating threat is that data subjects can invoke the legal 'right to be forgotten' (GDPR, CCPA, APPI, Canada's CPPA), yet simply deleting a user's records from the training set is insufficient because an adversary can still recover that information from the trained model. Concretely cited attack surfaces include membership inference attacks, model inversion attacks, and (for LLMs) leakage of private or copyrighted training data and generation of harmful content. Retraining from scratch is the ideal-but-infeasible defense (too costly; impossible in federated settings where the raw data is not accessible), so unlearning is positioned as the practical mechanism to remove a datum's influence while preserving utility. Note: this threat framing is drawn directly from the paper's own Introduction/Motivations, not imposed externally.
limitations: As a self-described 'short survey' the depth is limited: only ~75 references and ~14 core methods, with the whole Model-based MU section (Sec. 5) largely deferring to other surveys rather than analyzing methods in depth, and the parameter-agnostic branch resting on essentially a single method (ICuL). It contributes no experiments, no reproducible benchmarking, and no head-to-head quantitative comparison of the surveyed methods, so claims of relative effectiveness are qualitative. Coverage cutoff is early 2024 (arXiv v1, April 2024), so post-2024 unlearning benchmarks are absent. The manuscript also has notable proofreading issues (typos, an incorrect 2018 template citation year), and while it claims novelty over prior surveys, much of the traditional-model taxonomy visibly follows [62] and [34].

datasets_used: None used by the survey itself (no experiments). The only concrete dataset described is TOFU, mentioned only via cited work [39] Maini et al. 2024: 200 synthetic author profiles, each with 20 question-answer pairs, plus a 'forget set' subset as the unlearning target — this is NOT the survey author's own dataset.
quantitative_results: The survey reports no experimental numbers of its own. The only quantitative items are metric definitions it catalogs, e.g., Membership Inference recall = (#detected items / #forget items); ZRF score = 1 - (1/n_f) * sum_i JS(M(x_i), T_d(x_i)); Anamnesis Index AIN = rt(M_u,M_orig,alpha) / rt(M_s,M_orig,alpha). No accuracy/time/similarity values are measured or tabulated by the author.
baselines_compared: None — no empirical baseline comparison is run. Conceptually it uses retraining-from-scratch R(D_r) as the gold-standard reference that unlearning should approximate, and contrasts exact vs. approximate unlearning (Table 4). It positions itself against prior surveys [62] Xu et al. 2023 and [34] Li et al. 2024 as the works it extends toward LLMs.
